title: Polycythaemia Vera
oneLiner: A chronic myeloproliferative neoplasm causing erythropoietin-independent erythrocytosis (JAK2 V617F mutation), driving hyperviscosity, thrombosis, and risk of leukaemic transformation.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You are bleeped to see a 68-year-old man admitted with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and slurred speech. His wife says he’s been “off-colour” for months — headaches, high blood pressure, and intense itching after every hot shower. On examination: face is florid (plethora), conjunctivae injected, spleen palpable 4 cm below costal margin. BP 165/95, SpO₂ 97% on air. Full blood count shows haemoglobin 188 g/L, haematocrit 0.56, and a normal platelet count. He smokes. A CT head confirms acute ischaemic stroke.

**Dissect the vignette – each clue and what it tells you:**
- **Elderly, male, smoker** → typical demographic for PV (peak 60–70s); smoking adds hypoxic drive but alone can’t push Hct to 0.56.
- **Headaches, dizziness, fatigue** → hyperviscosity syndrome → impaired cerebral blood flow.
- **Pruritus after hot bath** → histamine release from basophils (histamine-mediated pruritus is pathognomonic for PV).
- **Plethora + injected conjunctivae** → high red cell mass; increased deoxygenated haemoglobin in capillaries.
- **Palpable splenomegaly** → extramedullary haematopoiesis; suggests myeloproliferative process.
- **Hypertension** → increased blood viscosity → peripheral resistance rises; also renal microthrombi.
- **Normal SpO₂** → rules out hypoxic (secondary) erythrocytosis.
- **Stroke as first presentation** → thrombotic complication is the commonest presenting event.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Isolated splanchnic vein thrombosis** (Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosis) in a young patient with no other signs – PV can be occult. Always check JAK2 in unexplained hepatic vein thrombosis.
- **Erythromelalgia**: burning pain, redness, and warmth in fingers/toes (microvascular platelet plugging) – mimics peripheral neuropathy or Raynaud’s.
- **Bleeding** (epistaxis, GI haemorrhage) due to acquired von Willebrand disease from extreme thrombocytosis (often masking the erythrocytosis).
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Polycythaemia Vera is a disorder where your bone marrow produces too many red blood cells (and often white cells and platelets) because of a permanent mutation in a single stem cell. It is a *myeloproliferative neoplasm* — a clonal disease, not just a reactive increase. The excess blood thickens, sludges in small vessels, and dramatically raises the risk of clots (stroke, heart attack, DVT, pulmonary embolism) and bleeding.

**Why this matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** incidence ~2–3/100,000/year; not rare in general medicine.
- **Stakes:** untreated, median survival ~2 years (mostly from thrombosis); with modern treatment, >15 years – but risk of progression to myelofibrosis or AML remains (10–20% over 20 years).
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosing as secondary erythrocytosis (COPD, sleep apnoea, high-altitude) and ordering expensive irrelevant tests; or failing to lower haematocrit aggressively enough (target <0.45). Missing the JAK2 mutation test.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**Tell the story:** A single haematopoietic stem cell acquires a gain-of-function mutation in the *JAK2* gene (95% of cases — V617F point mutation). The JAK2 protein is a tyrosine kinase that sits inside the cell, downstream of the erythropoietin (EPO) receptor, thrombopoietin receptor, and G-CSF receptor. The mutation makes JAK2 constitutively active – the cell thinks it’s always receiving growth factor signals, even when EPO is low or absent.

- **Red cell surge:** EPO-independent erythroid progenitor proliferation. Haematocrit climbs → blood viscosity increases (exponentially above 0.50). Hyperviscosity → reduced perfusion → headache, dizziness, visual disturbance.
- **White cell and platelet rise:** Often mild (granulocytosis, thrombocytosis). Excess platelets are dysfunctional – can both aggregate (causing thrombosis) and paradoxically cause bleeding due to acquired von Willebrand factor loss when platelet count >1000 × 10⁹/L.
- **Pruritus:** Basophils are part of the clone; they over-accumulate and degranulate histamine when temperature drops after a warm bath (temperature-dependent degranulation).
- **Splenomegaly:** Red pulp expands with extramedullary haematopoiesis (the marrow is crowded, and the spleen becomes a haematopoietic organ).
- **Hypertension:** Viscosity-dependent; also renal microthrombi activate renin-angiotensin.
- **Increased risk of thrombosis:** Viscosity + activated platelets + endothelial damage from vessel distension. Arterial > venous, but both common.

**Connect pathophys to pattern recognition cues:**
- Plethora = high Hct → capillaries full of desaturated Hb.
- Itchy after bath = histamine from basophils.
- Splenomegaly = extramedullary haematopoiesis.
- Stroke = hyperviscosity + platelet activation.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
Polycythaemia Vera is **primary** erythrocytosis – the problem is inside the bone marrow. All secondary causes must be ruled out.

| Category | Cause | 1-line stem clue |
|----------|-------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic (primary)** | Polycythaemia Vera (JAK2 mutation) | “Normal SpO₂, normal EPO, spleen tip palpable” |
| **Hypoxic (secondary)** | COPD, sleep apnoea, high altitude, cyanotic heart disease | “SpO₂ < 92%, cor pulmonale, EPO high” |
| **EPO-secreting tumours** | Renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, cerebellar haemangioblastoma | “Paraneoplastic syndrome, abdominal mass, polycythaemia without splenomegaly” |
| **Haematological (secondary)** | High-affinity haemoglobin variants (rare), Chuvash polycythaemia (VHL mutation) | “FHx of polycythaemia, normal SpO₂, normal EPO (but family pattern)” |
| **Drug-induced** | Androgens, erythropoietin abuse (e.g., endurance athletes) | “History of anabolic steroids or marathon runner, normal spleen” |

**Most common cause of *pathological* erythrocytosis:** Polycythaemia Vera (primary). However, in clinical practice, secondary erythrocytosis from COPD or smoking is far more common overall – but these patients usually have Hct <0.55 and normal spleen.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (WHO 2022):** Requires all 3 major criteria, OR first 2 major + any minor.

| Major criteria | Thresholds |
|---------------|------------|
| 1. Haemoglobin >165 g/L (men) / >160 g/L (women) OR Hct >0.49 (men) / >0.48 (women) | – |
| 2. Bone marrow biopsy showing hypercellularity with panmyelosis (trilineage proliferation) | Often not done if JAK2 positive and clear clinical picture; but gold standard for myelofibrosis progression. |
| 3. JAK2 V617F or JAK2 exon 12 mutation | Single most important test – if positive, diagnosis confirmed (95% sensitivity). |

| Minor criteria |
|----------------|
| Low serum erythropoietin (EPO) level (usually <4 IU/L) |
| Endogenous erythroid colony formation *in vitro* (rarely done) |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms?** JAK2 V617F mutation positive + low EPO → PV is virtually certain. If JAK2 negative but high Hb/low EPO, consider JAK2 exon 12 mutation or other MPN.

**Full investigation battery:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| FBC + differential | Hb, Hct, WBC, platelets | Baseline, monitor response. | Hct >0.49 (men) / >0.48 (women); often mild leucocytosis; platelets may be >450. |
| Serum EPO | Low in PV (<4), high in secondary | Key differentiator. | PV: low; secondary: high; occasional normal (equivocal – then bone marrow needed). |
| JAK2 mutation (blood or marrow) | V617F or exon 12 | Gold-standard diagnostic. | Positive = PV. Negative (~5%) – consider CALR/MPL mutations (then essential thrombocythaemia/myelofibrosis). |
| ABG + pulse oximetry | SpO₂, pO₂, CO | Exclude hypoxic secondary. | If SpO₂ < 92% (or pO₂ < 60 mmHg) → secondary likely; if normal → supports PV. |
| Iron studies | Ferritin, iron, TIBC | Baseline; PV patients often iron-deficient (pica is common). | Low ferritin <30 ng/mL – treat iron cautiously (can worsen erythrocytosis). |
| U&E, LFTs | Renal/hepatic function | Screen for RCC or hepatoma (secondary). | Abnormal LFTs + mass → consider imaging. |
| Abdominal USS | Spleen size, liver, kidneys | Detect splenomegaly, exclude RCC/hepatoma. | Splenomegaly >13cm supports PV; hepatic/RCC mass suggests secondary. |
| Bone marrow aspirate + trephine | Cellularity, fibrosis, cytogenetics | If JAK2 negative, or to assess progression (myelofibrosis). | Hypercellular, panmyelosis; reticulin fibrosis in later stages. |

**Single test that effectively rules in PV (if low EPO):** JAK2 V617F positive. **Single test that rules out PV:** JAK2 negative + high EPO + normal spleen (likely secondary cause).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**A. Immediate (first hour) – for newly diagnosed or acute hyperviscosity**
- **Venesection:** remove 250–500 mL whole blood (depending on Hct, weight, urgency). Target Hct <0.45. In acute stroke, aim <0.42.
- **Aspirin 75 mg PO stat** (if no bleeding risk). Reduces thrombosis by 50% in high-risk PV (age >60, prior thrombosis).
- **IV fluids (0.9% saline 500 mL bolus)** if there is evidence of hyperviscosity (confusion, focal neurology, visual blurring) – dilutes blood.
- **Check platelets:** if >1000 × 10⁹/L, consider add-on cytoreduction (hydroxycarbamide) before aspirin (risk of bleeding from acquired von Willebrand disease).

**B. Ward (24–72 hours) – stabilisation and risk stratification**
- **Repeat venesection daily** until Hct <0.45 (or <0.42 in high cardiovascular risk).
- **Start cytoreduction** if any of: age >60 years, prior thrombosis, platelet >1500 × 10⁹/L, symptomatic splenomegaly, or poor tolerance of venesection.
  - First line: **Hydroxycarbamide 500 mg PO once daily** (escalate by 500 mg every 2 weeks to maintain Hct target). Typical maintenance: 500–1500 mg/day.
  - Alternative (if intolerant): **Interferon-α (pegylated)** 45–180 mcg SC weekly – often used in younger patients (<60) and pregnancy.
  - Second-line: **Busulfan** (older, leukaemogenic) or **Ruxolitinib** (JAK1/2 inhibitor for hydroxycarbamide-resistant/intolerant).
- **Start low-dose aspirin 75 mg PO daily** (unless platelets >1000 × 10⁹/L – check von Willebrand activity first; if low, aspirin withheld until platelet count reduced).
- **Cardiovascular risk reduction:** Statin, antihypertensive, smoking cessation.

**C. Long-term – maintenance and monitoring**
- **Target Hct:** <0.45 lifelong. Venesection as needed every 1–3 months.
- **Aspirin 75 mg daily** (lifelong) unless contraindicated.
- **Monitor FBC every 3–6 months** – watch for rising WBC (risk of transformation) or falling Hb (myelofibrosis).
- **Annual iron studies –** venesection depletes iron. Iron deficiency reduces red cell quality but also limits erythropoiesis – generally do not replete unless severe symptoms (pica, restless legs). If you give iron, it may transiently raise Hct – do so under venesection cover.
- **Bone marrow every 3–5 years** (or if clinical suspicion of progression – new splenomegaly, cytopenias, constitutional symptoms).
- **Long-term complication surveillance:**
  - Monitor for MF: new anaemia, leucoerythroblastic blood film, massive splenomegaly.
  - Monitor for AML: blasts >5% in blood or marrow → refer for haematological opinion.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not start aspirin if platelets >1000 × 10⁹/L** without first checking von Willebrand ristocetin cofactor activity (vWF:RCo). Aspirin can exacerbate bleeding.
- **Do not treat iron deficiency aggressively** – it is the body’s natural brake on erythropoiesis. Replenish iron only if symptomatic (pica, severe fatigue) and under careful Hct control.
- **Do not ignore rising WBC** – a white count >15 × 10⁹/L correlates with increased risk of thrombosis and leukaemic transformation. Consider intensifying cytoreduction.
- **Do not discharge without arranging a haematology follow-up** within 2–4 weeks. These patients need lifelong specialist care.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Polycythaemia Vera | Secondary erythrocytosis (hypoxic) | Essential thrombocythaemia | Chronic hypoxia (COPD) |
|---------|--------------------|------------------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Haematocrit** | Markedly elevated (>0.49) | Elevated (0.46–0.55) | Usually normal | Mildly elevated (0.45–0.52) |
| **Platelets** | Often raised (400–1000) | Normal | Very high (>450, often >1000) | Normal |
| **WBC** | Mildly raised | Normal | Normal or raised | Normal |
| **Splenomegaly** | Present in ~70% | Absent | Absent (late may appear) | Absent |
| **EPO level** | Low | High (or normal if mild) | Normal | High (hypoxic stimulus) |
| **JAK2 mutation** | Positive (95%) | Negative | Positive in ~50% (but presents with thrombocytosis, not erythrocytosis) | Negative |
| **Pruritus** | Common (after bath) | Rare | Rare | Not a feature |
| **SpO₂** | Normal on air | Low (<92%) | Normal | Low (<92%) |
| **Arterial blood gas** | pO₂ normal | pO₂ low | Normal | pO₂ low, pCO₂ may be high |
| **Key differentiating test** | JAK2 + low EPO | EPO high + low SpO₂ | JAK2 mutation + thrombocytosis, normal Hct | ABG + lung function |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Arterial thrombosis** (stroke, MI, limb ischaemia) | Acute focal neurology, chest pain, cold pulseless limb | Hct >0.50, neurological deficit or ECG changes | Urgent CT/MRI, venesection to Hct <0.42, aspirin, manage per stroke/MI guidelines. Haematology consult. |
| **Venous thromboembolism** (DVT, PE, portal vein, Budd-Chiari) | Unilateral leg swelling, sudden dyspnoea, abdominal pain with ascites | DVT: calf tenderness; PE: hypoxia, pleuritic pain; Budd-Chiari: hepatomegaly, ascites | Anticoagulation (LMWH) + venesection. In Budd-Chiari, urgent TIPSS or surgical shunt. |
| **Acute hyperviscosity syndrome** | Confusion, headache, visual blurring, papilloedema, seizures | Hct >0.60, retinal vein engorgement, GCS <15 | Emergent venesection (500 mL stat), IV fluids, consider exchange transfusion if severe. |
| **Haemorrhagic complications** | Epistaxis, GI bleeding, easy bruising | Platelets >1000 × 10⁹/L, prolonged bleeding time, low vWF:RCo | Withhold aspirin. Perform venesection + urgent cytoreduction (hydroxycarbamide or anagrelide). Check vWF:RCo. |
| **Myelofibrosis (post-PV MF)** | Progressive anaemia, massive splenomegaly, night sweats, weight loss | Leucoerythroblastic film, tear-drop cells, red cell aniso-poikilocytosis, rising LDH, bone marrow fibrosis | Switch to Ruxolitinib or consider allogeneic stem cell transplant if fit and <70 years. |
| **Acute leukaemic transformation** (AML) | Fatigue, fever, bruising, bone pain | Blasts >20% in blood or marrow, cytopenias, splenomegaly | Refer to tertiary haematology. Immediate cytoreduction, consider chemotherapy or transplant. |

**Late-presentation patterns (the “what if untreated” stems):**  
- Patient with 5-year history of undiagnosed PV presents with massive splenomegaly (extends to iliac crest), anaemia (Hb 90), and blasts on film → post-PV myelofibrosis with transformation.
- Young woman presenting with acute Budd-Chiari (ascites, hepatomegaly, abnormal LFTs) – JAK2 positive → PV was the occult cause.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **P V = P rivitus, V iscosity, P lethora, V enesection, P latelets, V enous thrombosis**  
  (Most common features: Pruritus, Viscosity symptoms, Plethora, need for Venesection; also watch Platelets and Venous thrombosis.)
- **JAK2 = J ust A nother K inase causing E rythrocytosis** (for the mutation target)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Pruritus after hot bath is pathognomonic for PV** – not seen in secondary erythrocytosis. Ask: “Does the itching start when you shower and stop when you dry off?” If yes, think JAK2.
2. **Hct >0.60 is a haematological emergency** – risk of cardiopulmonary collapse; venesect immediately.
3. **Low EPO differentiates PV from secondary** – if EPO is high, it is NOT PV (unless unusual VHL mutation). If EPO is normal, you need a bone marrow.
4. **Platelet count >1000 × 10⁹/L in PV is a hypocritical finding** – paradoxically increases bleeding risk. Do not give aspirin until acquired von Willebrand disease excluded.
5. **Iron deficiency complicates venesection** – monitor ferritin; iron supplementation can cause a rebound Hct spike. Only give iron if symptomatic pica severe.
6. **Splenomegaly that becomes massively larger over months** may be the first sign of myelofibrosis, even with stable Hct.
7. **Pregnancy in PV is high-risk** – foetal loss, thrombosis, bleeding. Manage with interferon (not hydroxycarbamide) and aspirin. Refer to obstetric haematology.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**
- Ordering EPO without checking SpO₂ first (waste of money).
- Assuming all erythrocytosis is PV – always rule out hypoxia, smoking, and tumour.
- Starting hydroxycarbamide without checking for pregnancy (teratogenic).
- Ignoring rising WBC – a white count >15 predicts transformation.
- Discharging patient after venesection without starting aspirin (if no contraindication).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call a senior (registrar/consultant):**

| Situation | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or NEWS2 ≥ 3 in single parameter (e.g., SpO₂ < 91%, SBP < 90) | Any | Immediate review, consider underlying hyperviscosity syndrome, sepsis (may be masked). |
| **Hct >0.60** | Any | Call haematology registrar. May need exchange transfusion. |
| **Acute stroke** | Within 4.5 hours | Call stroke team, but also haematology (venesection may be needed before IV thrombolysis). |
| **Acute limb ischaemia** | Pulseless, pallor, paraesthesia | Vascular surgeon + haematology (venesection + LMWH). |
| **Budd-Chiari syndrome** | Ascites + tender hepatomegaly | Gastroenterology + haematology (may need TIPSS). |
| **Platelet count >1500 × 10⁹/L** with bleeding or planned surgery | Any | Discuss cytoreduction and von Willebrand testing with haematology registrar. |
| **GCS < 15** | Unexplained | Exclude hyperviscosity coma / intracranial bleed. |
| **Serum lactate > 2.5 mmol/L** | With unexplained metabolic acidosis | Suspect hyperviscosity-induced tissue ischaemia; venesect and escalate. |
| **Failure to achieve Hct < 0.45 after 3 venesections** | Under specialist advice | May require urgent cytoreduction or switch to Ruxolitinib. |

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- **Cardiorespiratory arrest** – standard ALS.
- **Severe hyperviscosity syndrome** (seizures, coma, respiratory distress) – consider exchange transfusion, intubation for airway protection.
- **Massive PE** with haemodynamic instability – thrombolysis + haematology input.

**Escalation tip:** In any patient with known PV who deteriorates unexpectedly, always check Hct, platelets, and coagulation first – hyperviscosity can mimic sepsis. Do not wait for full investigations if Hct >0.60 – venesect urgently, then discuss.

## Polycythaemia Vera

**One-liner:** A chronic myeloproliferative neoplasm causing erythropoietin-independent erythrocytosis (JAK2 V617F mutation), driving hyperviscosity, thrombosis, and risk of leukaemic transformation.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See

You are bleeped to see a 68-year-old man admitted with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and slurred speech. His wife says he’s been “off-colour” for months — headaches, high blood pressure, and intense itching after every hot shower. On examination: face is florid (plethora), conjunctivae injected, spleen palpable 4 cm below costal margin. BP 165/95, SpO₂ 97% on air. Full blood count shows haemoglobin 188 g/L, haematocrit 0.56, and a normal platelet count. He smokes. A CT head confirms acute ischaemic stroke.

**Dissect the vignette – each clue and what it tells you:**
- **Elderly, male, smoker** → typical demographic for PV (peak 60–70s); smoking adds hypoxic drive but alone can’t push Hct to 0.56.
- **Headaches, dizziness, fatigue** → hyperviscosity syndrome → impaired cerebral blood flow.
- **Pruritus after hot bath** → histamine release from basophils (histamine-mediated pruritus is pathognomonic for PV).
- **Plethora + injected conjunctivae** → high red cell mass; increased deoxygenated haemoglobin in capillaries.
- **Palpable splenomegaly** → extramedullary haematopoiesis; suggests myeloproliferative process.
- **Hypertension** → increased blood viscosity → peripheral resistance rises; also renal microthrombi.
- **Normal SpO₂** → rules out hypoxic (secondary) erythrocytosis.
- **Stroke as first presentation** → thrombotic complication is the commonest presenting event.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Isolated splanchnic vein thrombosis** (Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosis) in a young patient with no other signs – PV can be occult. Always check JAK2 in unexplained hepatic vein thrombosis.
- **Erythromelalgia**: burning pain, redness, and warmth in fingers/toes (microvascular platelet plugging) – mimics peripheral neuropathy or Raynaud’s.
- **Bleeding** (epistaxis, GI haemorrhage) due to acquired von Willebrand disease from extreme thrombocytosis (often masking the erythrocytosis).

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Polycythaemia Vera is a disorder where your bone marrow produces too many red blood cells (and often white cells and platelets) because of a permanent mutation in a single stem cell. It is a *myeloproliferative neoplasm* — a clonal disease, not just a reactive increase. The excess blood thickens, sludges in small vessels, and dramatically raises the risk of clots (stroke, heart attack, DVT, pulmonary embolism) and bleeding.

**Why this matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** incidence ~2–3/100,000/year; not rare in general medicine.
- **Stakes:** untreated, median survival ~2 years (mostly from thrombosis); with modern treatment, >15 years – but risk of progression to myelofibrosis or AML remains (10–20% over 20 years).
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosing as secondary erythrocytosis (COPD, sleep apnoea, high-altitude) and ordering expensive irrelevant tests; or failing to lower haematocrit aggressively enough (target <0.45). Missing the JAK2 mutation test.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**Tell the story:** A single haematopoietic stem cell acquires a gain-of-function mutation in the *JAK2* gene (95% of cases — V617F point mutation). The JAK2 protein is a tyrosine kinase that sits inside the cell, downstream of the erythropoietin (EPO) receptor, thrombopoietin receptor, and G-CSF receptor. The mutation makes JAK2 constitutively active – the cell thinks it’s always receiving growth factor signals, even when EPO is low or absent.

- **Red cell surge:** EPO-independent erythroid progenitor proliferation. Haematocrit climbs → blood viscosity increases (exponentially above 0.50). Hyperviscosity → reduced perfusion → headache, dizziness, visual disturbance.
- **White cell and platelet rise:** Often mild (granulocytosis, thrombocytosis). Excess platelets are dysfunctional – can both aggregate (causing thrombosis) and paradoxically cause bleeding due to acquired von Willebrand factor loss when platelet count >1000 × 10⁹/L.
- **Pruritus:** Basophils are part of the clone; they over-accumulate and degranulate histamine when temperature drops after a warm bath (temperature-dependent degranulation).
- **Splenomegaly:** Red pulp expands with extramedullary haematopoiesis (the marrow is crowded, and the spleen becomes a haematopoietic organ).
- **Hypertension:** Viscosity-dependent; also renal microthrombi activate renin-angiotensin.
- **Increased risk of thrombosis:** Viscosity + activated platelets + endothelial damage from vessel distension. Arterial > venous, but both common.

**Connect pathophys to pattern recognition cues:**
- Plethora = high Hct → capillaries full of desaturated Hb.
- Itchy after bath = histamine from basophils.
- Splenomegaly = extramedullary haematopoiesis.
- Stroke = hyperviscosity + platelet activation.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

Polycythaemia Vera is **primary** erythrocytosis – the problem is inside the bone marrow. All secondary causes must be ruled out.

| Category | Cause | 1-line stem clue |
|----------|-------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic (primary)** | Polycythaemia Vera (JAK2 mutation) | “Normal SpO₂, normal EPO, spleen tip palpable” |
| **Hypoxic (secondary)** | COPD, sleep apnoea, high altitude, cyanotic heart disease | “SpO₂ < 92%, cor pulmonale, EPO high” |
| **EPO-secreting tumours** | Renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, cerebellar haemangioblastoma | “Paraneoplastic syndrome, abdominal mass, polycythaemia without splenomegaly” |
| **Haematological (secondary)** | High-affinity haemoglobin variants (rare), Chuvash polycythaemia (VHL mutation) | “FHx of polycythaemia, normal SpO₂, normal EPO (but family pattern)” |
| **Drug-induced** | Androgens, erythropoietin abuse (e.g., endurance athletes) | “History of anabolic steroids or marathon runner, normal spleen” |

**Most common cause of *pathological* erythrocytosis:** Polycythaemia Vera (primary). However, in clinical practice, secondary erythrocytosis from COPD or smoking is far more common overall – but these patients usually have Hct <0.55 and normal spleen.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (WHO 2022):** Requires all 3 major criteria, OR first 2 major + any minor.

| Major criteria | Thresholds |
|---------------|------------|
| 1. Haemoglobin >165 g/L (men) / >160 g/L (women) OR Hct >0.49 (men) / >0.48 (women) | – |
| 2. Bone marrow biopsy showing hypercellularity with panmyelosis (trilineage proliferation) | Often not done if JAK2 positive and clear clinical picture; but gold standard for myelofibrosis progression. |
| 3. JAK2 V617F or JAK2 exon 12 mutation | Single most important test – if positive, diagnosis confirmed (95% sensitivity). |

| Minor criteria |
|----------------|
| Low serum erythropoietin (EPO) level (usually <4 IU/L) |
| Endogenous erythroid colony formation *in vitro* (rarely done) |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms?** JAK2 V617F mutation positive + low EPO → PV is virtually certain. If JAK2 negative but high Hb/low EPO, consider JAK2 exon 12 mutation or other MPN.

**Full investigation battery:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| FBC + differential | Hb, Hct, WBC, platelets | Baseline, monitor response. | Hct >0.49 (men) / >0.48 (women); often mild leucocytosis; platelets may be >450. |
| Serum EPO | Low in PV (<4), high in secondary | Key differentiator. | PV: low; secondary: high; occasional normal (equivocal – then bone marrow needed). |
| JAK2 mutation (blood or marrow) | V617F or exon 12 | Gold-standard diagnostic. | Positive = PV. Negative (~5%) – consider CALR/MPL mutations (then essential thrombocythaemia/myelofibrosis). |
| ABG + pulse oximetry | SpO₂, pO₂, CO | Exclude hypoxic secondary. | If SpO₂ < 92% (or pO₂ < 60 mmHg) → secondary likely; if normal → supports PV. |
| Iron studies | Ferritin, iron, TIBC | Baseline; PV patients often iron-deficient (pica is common). | Low ferritin <30 ng/mL – treat iron cautiously (can worsen erythrocytosis). |
| U&E, LFTs | Renal/hepatic function | Screen for RCC or hepatoma (secondary). | Abnormal LFTs + mass → consider imaging. |
| Abdominal USS | Spleen size, liver, kidneys | Detect splenomegaly, exclude RCC/hepatoma. | Splenomegaly >13cm supports PV; hepatic/RCC mass suggests secondary. |
| Bone marrow aspirate + trephine | Cellularity, fibrosis, cytogenetics | If JAK2 negative, or to assess progression (myelofibrosis). | Hypercellular, panmyelosis; reticulin fibrosis in later stages. |

**Single test that effectively rules in PV (if low EPO):** JAK2 V617F positive. **Single test that rules out PV:** JAK2 negative + high EPO + normal spleen (likely secondary cause).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**A. Immediate (first hour) – for newly diagnosed or acute hyperviscosity**
- **Venesection:** remove 250–500 mL whole blood (depending on Hct, weight, urgency). Target Hct <0.45. In acute stroke, aim <0.42.
- **Aspirin 75 mg PO stat** (if no bleeding risk). Reduces thrombosis by 50% in high-risk PV (age >60, prior thrombosis).
- **IV fluids (0.9% saline 500 mL bolus)** if there is evidence of hyperviscosity (confusion, focal neurology, visual blurring) – dilutes blood.
- **Check platelets:** if >1000 × 10⁹/L, consider add-on cytoreduction (hydroxycarbamide) before aspirin (risk of bleeding from acquired von Willebrand disease).

**B. Ward (24–72 hours) – stabilisation and risk stratification**
- **Repeat venesection daily** until Hct <0.45 (or <0.42 in high cardiovascular risk).
- **Start cytoreduction** if any of: age >60 years, prior thrombosis, platelet >1500 × 10⁹/L, symptomatic splenomegaly, or poor tolerance of venesection.
  - First line: **Hydroxycarbamide 500 mg PO once daily** (escalate by 500 mg every 2 weeks to maintain Hct target). Typical maintenance: 500–1500 mg/day.
  - Alternative (if intolerant): **Interferon-α (pegylated)** 45–180 mcg SC weekly – often used in younger patients (<60) and pregnancy.
  - Second-line: **Busulfan** (older, leukaemogenic) or **Ruxolitinib** (JAK1/2 inhibitor for hydroxycarbamide-resistant/intolerant).
- **Start low-dose aspirin 75 mg PO daily** (unless platelets >1000 × 10⁹/L – check von Willebrand activity first; if low, aspirin withheld until platelet count reduced).
- **Cardiovascular risk reduction:** Statin, antihypertensive, smoking cessation.

**C. Long-term – maintenance and monitoring**
- **Target Hct:** <0.45 lifelong. Venesection as needed every 1–3 months.
- **Aspirin 75 mg daily** (lifelong) unless contraindicated.
- **Monitor FBC every 3–6 months** – watch for rising WBC (risk of transformation) or falling Hb (myelofibrosis).
- **Annual iron studies –** venesection depletes iron. Iron deficiency reduces red cell quality but also limits erythropoiesis – generally do not replete unless severe symptoms (pica, restless legs). If you give iron, it may transiently raise Hct – do so under venesection cover.
- **Bone marrow every 3–5 years** (or if clinical suspicion of progression – new splenomegaly, cytopenias, constitutional symptoms).
- **Long-term complication surveillance:**
  - Monitor for MF: new anaemia, leucoerythroblastic blood film, massive splenomegaly.
  - Monitor for AML: blasts >5% in blood or marrow → refer for haematological opinion.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not start aspirin if platelets >1000 × 10⁹/L** without first checking von Willebrand ristocetin cofactor activity (vWF:RCo). Aspirin can exacerbate bleeding.
- **Do not treat iron deficiency aggressively** – it is the body’s natural brake on erythropoiesis. Replenish iron only if symptomatic (pica, severe fatigue) and under careful Hct control.
- **Do not ignore rising WBC** – a white count >15 × 10⁹/L correlates with increased risk of thrombosis and leukaemic transformation. Consider intensifying cytoreduction.
- **Do not discharge without arranging a haematology follow-up** within 2–4 weeks. These patients need lifelong specialist care.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Polycythaemia Vera | Secondary erythrocytosis (hypoxic) | Essential thrombocythaemia | Chronic hypoxia (COPD) |
|---------|--------------------|------------------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Haematocrit** | Markedly elevated (>0.49) | Elevated (0.46–0.55) | Usually normal | Mildly elevated (0.45–0.52) |
| **Platelets** | Often raised (400–1000) | Normal | Very high (>450, often >1000) | Normal |
| **WBC** | Mildly raised | Normal | Normal or raised | Normal |
| **Splenomegaly** | Present in ~70% | Absent | Absent (late may appear) | Absent |
| **EPO level** | Low | High (or normal if mild) | Normal | High (hypoxic stimulus) |
| **JAK2 mutation** | Positive (95%) | Negative | Positive in ~50% (but presents with thrombocytosis, not erythrocytosis) | Negative |
| **Pruritus** | Common (after bath) | Rare | Rare | Not a feature |
| **SpO₂** | Normal on air | Low (<92%) | Normal | Low (<92%) |
| **Arterial blood gas** | pO₂ normal | pO₂ low | Normal | pO₂ low, pCO₂ may be high |
| **Key differentiating test** | JAK2 + low EPO | EPO high + low SpO₂ | JAK2 mutation + thrombocytosis, normal Hct | ABG + lung function |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Arterial thrombosis** (stroke, MI, limb ischaemia) | Acute focal neurology, chest pain, cold pulseless limb | Hct >0.50, neurological deficit or ECG changes | Urgent CT/MRI, venesection to Hct <0.42, aspirin, manage per stroke/MI guidelines. Haematology consult. |
| **Venous thromboembolism** (DVT, PE, portal vein, Budd-Chiari) | Unilateral leg swelling, sudden dyspnoea, abdominal pain with ascites | DVT: calf tenderness; PE: hypoxia, pleuritic pain; Budd-Chiari: hepatomegaly, ascites | Anticoagulation (LMWH) + venesection. In Budd-Chiari, urgent TIPSS or surgical shunt. |
| **Acute hyperviscosity syndrome** | Confusion, headache, visual blurring, papilloedema, seizures | Hct >0.60, retinal vein engorgement, GCS <15 | Emergent venesection (500 mL stat), IV fluids, consider exchange transfusion if severe. |
| **Haemorrhagic complications** | Epistaxis, GI bleeding, easy bruising | Platelets >1000 × 10⁹/L, prolonged bleeding time, low vWF:RCo | Withhold aspirin. Perform venesection + urgent cytoreduction (hydroxycarbamide or anagrelide). Check vWF:RCo. |
| **Myelofibrosis (post-PV MF)** | Progressive anaemia, massive splenomegaly, night sweats, weight loss | Leucoerythroblastic film, tear-drop cells, red cell aniso-poikilocytosis, rising LDH, bone marrow fibrosis | Switch to Ruxolitinib or consider allogeneic stem cell transplant if fit and <70 years. |
| **Acute leukaemic transformation** (AML) | Fatigue, fever, bruising, bone pain | Blasts >20% in blood or marrow, cytopenias, splenomegaly | Refer to tertiary haematology. Immediate cytoreduction, consider chemotherapy or transplant. |

**Late-presentation patterns (the “what if untreated” stems):**  
- Patient with 5-year history of undiagnosed PV presents with massive splenomegaly (extends to iliac crest), anaemia (Hb 90), and blasts on film → post-PV myelofibrosis with transformation.
- Young woman presenting with acute Budd-Chiari (ascites, hepatomegaly, abnormal LFTs) – JAK2 positive → PV was the occult cause.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **P V = P rivitus, V iscosity, P lethora, V enesection, P latelets, V enous thrombosis**  
  (Most common features: Pruritus, Viscosity symptoms, Plethora, need for Venesection; also watch Platelets and Venous thrombosis.)
- **JAK2 = J ust A nother K inase causing E rythrocytosis** (for the mutation target)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Pruritus after hot bath is pathognomonic for PV** – not seen in secondary erythrocytosis. Ask: “Does the itching start when you shower and stop when you dry off?” If yes, think JAK2.
2. **Hct >0.60 is a haematological emergency** – risk of cardiopulmonary collapse; venesect immediately.
3. **Low EPO differentiates PV from secondary** – if EPO is high, it is NOT PV (unless unusual VHL mutation). If EPO is normal, you need a bone marrow.
4. **Platelet count >1000 × 10⁹/L in PV is a hypocritical finding** – paradoxically increases bleeding risk. Do not give aspirin until acquired von Willebrand disease excluded.
5. **Iron deficiency complicates venesection** – monitor ferritin; iron supplementation can cause a rebound Hct spike. Only give iron if symptomatic pica severe.
6. **Splenomegaly that becomes massively larger over months** may be the first sign of myelofibrosis, even with stable Hct.
7. **Pregnancy in PV is high-risk** – foetal loss, thrombosis, bleeding. Manage with interferon (not hydroxycarbamide) and aspirin. Refer to obstetric haematology.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**
- Ordering EPO without checking SpO₂ first (waste of money).
- Assuming all erythrocytosis is PV – always rule out hypoxia, smoking, and tumour.
- Starting hydroxycarbamide without checking for pregnancy (teratogenic).
- Ignoring rising WBC – a white count >15 predicts transformation.
- Discharging patient after venesection without starting aspirin (if no contraindication).

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call a senior (registrar/consultant):**

| Situation | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or NEWS2 ≥ 3 in single parameter (e.g., SpO₂ < 91%, SBP < 90) | Any | Immediate review, consider underlying hyperviscosity syndrome, sepsis (may be masked). |
| **Hct >0.60** | Any | Call haematology registrar. May need exchange transfusion. |
| **Acute stroke** | Within 4.5 hours | Call stroke team, but also haematology (venesection may be needed before IV thrombolysis). |
| **Acute limb ischaemia** | Pulseless, pallor, paraesthesia | Vascular surgeon + haematology (venesection + LMWH). |
| **Budd-Chiari syndrome** | Ascites + tender hepatomegaly | Gastroenterology + haematology (may need TIPSS). |
| **Platelet count >1500 × 10⁹/L** with bleeding or planned surgery | Any | Discuss cytoreduction and von Willebrand testing with haematology registrar. |
| **GCS < 15** | Unexplained | Exclude hyperviscosity coma / intracranial bleed. |
| **Serum lactate > 2.5 mmol/L** | With unexplained metabolic acidosis | Suspect hyperviscosity-induced tissue ischaemia; venesect and escalate. |
| **Failure to achieve Hct < 0.45 after 3 venesections** | Under specialist advice | May require urgent cytoreduction or switch to Ruxolitinib. |

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- **Cardiorespiratory arrest** – standard ALS.
- **Severe hyperviscosity syndrome** (seizures, coma, respiratory distress) – consider exchange transfusion, intubation for airway protection.
- **Massive PE** with haemodynamic instability – thrombolysis + haematology input.

**Escalation tip:** In any patient with known PV who deteriorates unexpectedly, always check Hct, platelets, and coagulation first – hyperviscosity can mimic sepsis. Do not wait for full investigations if Hct >0.60 – venesect urgently, then discuss.
